next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

They'd been at it for three weeks, four days a week, at Doug's kitchen table. Simone had given up on jokes somewhere in the first week, and what they did now didn't have a name, or at least not one Doug recognized from any category of professional work he'd ever participated in. She asked him questions. He answered them. She decided which answers were something and which were nothing, and the criteria for that decision remained, to Doug, as opaque as the underwriting guidelines Mutual Fidelity revised every January without telling anyone what had changed.

The recorder sat between them — a small silver thing she'd carried from New York, scratched along one edge, a strip of masking tape on the back where she'd written "WORK" in black marker, though Doug didn't know what other kind of recording she might need to distinguish it from. She turned it on every morning when she arrived at nine and off again somewhere around one or two, and in between she listened in a way that made Doug hear his own voice in pieces — the pauses, the filler words he hadn't known he used, the places where a sentence died and he had to start a new one from scratch.

The table was the same one he'd bought from the IKEA in West Chester twelve years ago when he'd moved into the apartment. Birch veneer, four legs, no distinguishing features. It had a water ring from a mug he'd set down sometime in 2016 and never dealt with. Simone put her coffee on the same spot every morning, directly on the ring. Doug noticed and said nothing. Saying something would mean admitting he noticed where she put...
open_hooks_to_carry: Gloria's notes on the video — delivered without preamble, mixing harsh and perceptive
The 'only real thing you've said since you were twelve' line — Gloria's highest standard applied to Doug
prev_tail_excerpt:
"You should get some sleep," Roy said.

"Yeah."

"Drive back in the morning. Don't drive tonight."

"I won't."

A pause. Then Roy said, "Doug."

"Yeah."

"Nineteen minutes is nineteen more than most people manage."

Doug wasn't sure if this was comfort or observation or just Roy's way of placing a fact in its correct location before closing the file. With Roy, the distinction was often academic.

"Good night, Roy."

"Night."

Doug hung up and set his phone on the nightstand next to the bolted lamp. He took off his shoes and dress shirt and lay on the bedspread in his undershirt and pants. The remote was under his hip. He left it there.

He thought about the woman checking her phone. The cough. Brian saying *the man, the legend*. He thought about the toast. At the wedding, nobody had been braced for him, including him. Tonight everybody had.

Outside the window, traffic moved along West Washington Street. The air conditioner rattled and caught. The fifteen thousand dollars would arrive by direct deposit within fourteen business days.

After a minute he pulled back the covers and got under them.

Down the hall, the ice machine dumped a load into its bin. No one came for it.
state_deltas_required: Gloria's total refusal to manage Doug's feelings combined with the weight of family history and his dead father's shadow

Gloria provides an unmediated mirror — Doug sees himself as neither the internet's genius nor his own self-image as a failure, but a specific man with a specific inheritance of saying the wrong thing at the right time
